passages.explanation: Jesus goes out from the temple. His disciples come to show him the temple buildings. He tells them: don't you see all of these things? Most certainly I tell you, not one stone will be left on another that won't be thrown down. On the Mount of Olives, the disciples ask privately: when will these things be? What will be the sign of your coming and the end of the age? Jesus answers: be careful that no one leads you astray. Many will come in my name saying I am the Christ, and will lead many astray. You'll hear of wars and rumours of wars — don't be troubled. These things must happen, but the end is not yet. Nation will rise against nation, kingdom against kingdom. There will be famines, plagues, and earthquakes in various places. All these are the beginning of birth pains. The disciples assume the temple's destruction and the end of the age are the same event. Jesus begins untangling them — and his first warning isn't about signs but about deception.

Then they'll deliver you up to oppression, kill you, and you'll be hated by all nations for my name's sake. Many will stumble, betray one another, and hate one another. Many false prophets will arise and lead many astray. Because lawlessness will be multiplied, the love of many will grow cold. But whoever endures to the end will be saved. This Good News of the Kingdom will be preached in the whole world for a testimony to all the nations, and then the end will come. The danger isn't just external persecution — it's internal decay. When lawlessness increases, love doesn't fight back; it simply cools.

When you see the abomination of desolation, spoken of through Daniel the prophet, standing in the holy place — let the reader understand — then let those in Judea flee to the mountains. Don't go back for anything in your house. Don't turn back for your cloak. Woe to those who are pregnant and nursing in those days. Pray that your flight won't be in winter or on a Sabbath. There will be great oppression such as has not been from the beginning of the world until now, and never will be. Unless those days were shortened, no flesh would be saved — but for the sake of the chosen, those days will be shortened. If anyone says the Christ is here or there, don't believe it. False christs and false prophets will show great signs and wonders, enough to lead astray even the chosen, if possible. I've told you beforehand. If they say he's in the wilderness, don't go. If they say he's in the inner rooms, don't believe it. As the lightning flashes from east to west, so will the coming of the Son of Man be. Wherever the carcass is, there the vultures will gather. The primary reference is the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD — and it happened exactly as described. The language also telescopes forward to the final coming. Jesus' advice is practical and urgent: run, don't look back, don't be deceived.

Immediately after the oppression of those days, the sun will be darkened, the moon won't give its light, the stars will fall from the sky, and the powers of the heavens will be shaken. The sign of the Son of Man will appear in the sky. All the tribes of the earth will mourn. They'll see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of the sky with power and great glory. He'll send out his angels with a great sound of a trumpet, and they'll gather his chosen ones from the four winds. Learn this parable from the fig tree: when its branch becomes tender and produces leaves, you know summer is near. When you see all these things, know that it's near — at the doors. This generation won't pass away until all these things are accomplished. Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away. The cosmic imagery draws from Isaiah and Joel — it's prophetic language for world-shaking events. Jesus stakes his credibility on his words outlasting the universe itself.

But no one knows the day or hour — not even the angels of heaven, not even the Son — only the Father. As the days of Noah were, so will the coming of the Son of Man be. In the days before the flood, they were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage, until the day Noah entered the ark — and they didn't know until the flood came and took them all away. So will the coming of the Son of Man be. Two men will be in the field — one taken, one left. Two women grinding at the mill — one taken, one left. Watch therefore, for you don't know what hour your Lord comes. If the master of the house had known what watch of the night the thief was coming, he would have watched. Therefore be ready, for in an hour you don't expect, the Son of Man will come. The point isn't to calculate the date — it's to live in readiness. The danger is normalcy — life going on undisturbed until the interruption.

Who then is the faithful and wise servant, whom his lord has set over his household to give them their food in due season? Blessed is that servant whom his lord finds doing so when he comes. He'll set him over all that he has. But if that evil servant says in his heart: my lord is delayed — and begins to beat his fellow servants and eat and drink with the drunkards — his lord will come on a day he doesn't expect, at an hour he doesn't know. He'll cut him in pieces and appoint his portion with the hypocrites, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth. The parable tests what people do when they think no one is watching. Delayed return is not cancelled return — and the servant's true character shows in the waiting.
